Commissioner & Player Guide

NCAAF Survivor (Private)

Everything commissioners and entrants need to set up, run, and play an invite-only College Football Survivor pool — from picking a format to reading the final standings.

Contents

Jump to any section — this isn't a persistent sidebar, just a quick way in.

1Overview — What Survivor Is 2Setting Up a Contest 3My Entries & Making Picks 4Contest Settings — General 5Contest Settings — Picksheet & Eligibility 6Contest Settings — Pick Format 7Contest Settings — Lives & Revivals 8Contest Settings — Missed Picks & Auto-Pick 9Contest Settings — Tiebreakers 10Contest Settings — Groups & Design 11Entrants Manager & Invites Manager 12Leaderboards 13Missed Picks / Strikes Quick Reference 14FAQ 15Glossary
1Getting Started

Overview — What Survivor Is

NCAAF Survivor is a multi-week, invite-only elimination contest. Each week, every entry picks one team (or more, in multi-pick weeks) it believes will win. A correct pick means the entry survives to the next week; an incorrect pick costs a life. Run out of lives and the entry is eliminated — the last entry (or entries) standing wins. There are no points: surviving is everything.

The three building blocks every commissioner chooses

ChoiceOptionsWhat it means for the group
League / Sport ModeCollege Football Survivor, or NFL + NCAAF Survivor (combined)A pure college pool draws only from CFB games each week; a combined pool lets members pick from either league in the same contest.
Pick TypePick Winners or Pick LosersClassic Survivor picks teams to win. A "loser pool" inverts it: pick the team you think will LOSE — if it wins, you lose a life.
Scoring BasisStraight Up, Against the Spread (Splash-set), or Against the Spread (Commissioner-set)Straight Up only cares who wins. Against the Spread requires covering a point spread — either Splash's live odds or numbers the commissioner sets by hand.

What sets CFB Survivor apart

Because a college slate can include well over 100 FBS teams across ten conferences (plus FCS opponents and Top 25 matchups), CFB Survivor pools lean heavily on the Picksheet & Eligibility settings (Section 5) to shape which games and teams even show up — something NFL Survivor pools don't need since there are only 32 teams and one slate.

Quick facts CS/Sales can lead with

  • Invite-only — no entry-fee mechanic built into the contest itself.
  • No cap on contest size by default; the commissioner closes entries manually when they're ready.
  • Multi-entry is supported: default is 1 entry per person, and the commissioner can raise the per-person cap (up to 250).
  • Supports a full college season (Week 1 through the National Championship), or a blended NFL + CFB season.
  • One season-long standings view — alive entries on top, eliminated entries ranked by how long they survived. No weekly leaderboard.
  • Most commissioner settings can be changed mid-season; tiebreakers can be changed at any time, even mid-contest.
NoteThis guide covers the College Football (NCAAF) build of Survivor. Settings shared with NFL Survivor are described the same way here since the underlying mechanics don't change — only the picksheet, schedule, and a handful of CFB-specific filters differ.
↑ Back to top

Quick Check

1. What's the core mechanic that decides who's still in a Survivor pool?

2Commissioner Setup

Setting Up a Contest

Creating a contest takes three quick steps: pick the format, name it, and land on the commissioner's onboarding dashboard.

Step 1 — Choose a contest format

From the format picker, select the Survivor tab, then choose between the two CFB-relevant options:

  • College Football Survivor — a pure CFB pool.
  • NFL + NCAAF Survivor — a combined pool where members pick from either league each week.

Step 2 — Name the contest

Give the contest a name (this is the only required field at creation — description and logo can be added later from Contest Settings).

NoteCreating the contest triggers a one-time service fee when the contest starts, unless the commissioner sets up Contest Vault to waive it.

Step 3 — The onboarding dashboard

Once created, the commissioner lands on a welcome dashboard with entry stats at the top (entries available, invites sent, entered count) and a 4-step checklist:

  1. Contest created (done automatically)
  2. Set up entry fee collection (optionally waive the service fee via Contest Vault, or manage payments independently)
  3. Review contest settings (fine-tune rules, entries, and lock times before players join)
  4. Send invites (through Splash, or by copying the contest link)

Navigating the contest

The left-hand navigation is split into two groups: a top group for entrants (Contest details, My entries, Activity) visible to every member, and a Commissioner group (Dashboard, Contest settings, Entrants manager, Invites manager, Contest Vault) visible only to the person running the pool. Above both is the entries widget, showing the countdown to lock, total entries, and fill progress toward the max.

↑ Back to top

Quick Check

1. Which two Survivor formats are CFB-relevant in the format picker?

3. Where does a commissioner go to waive the one-time service fee?

3Player Experience

My Entries & Making Picks

Every member manages their picks from My entries. Each entry card shows lives remaining and lets the member rename, withdraw, or make picks for that entry.

Reading an entry card

  • The ❤️ heart icon shows how many lives the entry has left.
  • The ✏️ pencil icon next to the entry name opens a rename dialog.
  • Withdraw removes the entry from the contest permanently — there's no undo.
  • Make picks opens the picksheet for that entry.
WarnWithdrawing an entry is permanent. There's no confirmation step after the final click that can be undone — make sure the member actually wants to leave before withdrawing.

Making picks

The Make Picks screen highlights the current week at the top (with week tabs for the rest of the season) and shows the entry's remaining lives. Selecting a team fills that slot; the bottom bar tracks how many picks are still required for that week (e.g. "1/1 pick made").

Members don't need to lock in every pick at once — they can select a team now and come back before the deadline to change it. The one non-negotiable step is clicking Save picks every time; picks aren't registered in the contest until that button is clicked.

After saving, the back arrow returns to the entry view, where the entry card now shows a chip for each team picked so far.

Activity tab

The Activity tab is a chronological log of changes to the contest — commissioner settings updates, group membership moves, and pick modifications made on behalf of entrants. It's split into two sub-tabs:

Activity shows commissioner changes to the contest as they happen. Pick changes shows only the subset where the commissioner made a pick on behalf of an entrant (relevant when Hide Future Picks From Commissioner is off — see Section 4). Both start empty and populate as changes occur.

↑ Back to top

Quick Check

4Contest Settings

Contest Settings — General

The General tab covers the contest's identity, schedule, and the basic rules for how and when entries can join.

SettingDefaultWhat it does
Contest name / Description—Name is required at creation; description is optional (up to 500 characters).
Start week / End weekWeek 1 → CFB National ChampionshipDefines the first and last week of the contest. Setting the end week into the postseason is how a contest extends through the playoffs.
Maximum entries per entrant1Cap on entries per player. Commissioner can raise it (1–250).
Entry deadlineOffWhen on, sets a specific date/time after which no new entries are accepted.
Lock entries immediatelyOffStops new members from joining right away, regardless of the entry deadline — the fastest way to "cap" a pool once the group is set.
Hide Future Picks From CommissionerOnWhen on, the commissioner sees entries exactly as members do — only picks past their deadline. When off, the commissioner can see and edit everything.
TipSchedule runs Week 1 through the CFB National Championship for College-only and Combined contests. Extending the End Week into the postseason is the only way to carry a Survivor pool into the playoffs.
↑ Back to top

Quick Check

1. What does the CFB schedule run from and to by default?

2. What's the default state of Hide Future Picks From Commissioner?

3. What does Lock entries immediately do?

5Contest Settings

Contest Settings — Picksheet & Eligibility

Picksheet & Eligibility controls which teams and games appear on the weekly picksheet, and how often a team can be reused. Several of these filters exist only for College and Combined contests — they don't apply to NFL-only Survivor since the NFL slate doesn't need conference or ranking filters.

SettingDefaultWhat it does
Team use limitOnce (set via +/- stepper, up to Unlimited)How many times a single team can be picked across an entry's lifetime — the classic Survivor constraint.
Pick-against team limitUnlimitedCaps how often an entry can pick against (select the opponent of) a specific team. Applies to both Pick Winners and Pick Losers.
Playoff reset — team use & pick historyOffWhen the contest reaches the postseason, team-use counts and pick history reset so every team can be picked again. Only relevant if the End Week extends into the postseason.
TipPlayoff reset is one of the most popular settings commissioners enable for pools that carry into the postseason — it keeps the bracket stage from being decided purely by who ran out of teams first.

CFB / Combined-only picksheet filters

These six filters only appear for College and Combined contests, since they depend on conference structure and FCS/FBS classification that NFL Survivor doesn't have:

FilterDefaultWhat it does
Select ConferencesAll selected (at least one required)Which CFB conferences are eligible for the picksheet: SEC, Big Ten, Big 12, ACC, Mountain West, American Athletic, Conference USA, Mid-American, Sun Belt, Independents.
Include AP Top 25 GamesOffAdds games involving Top 25 teams even if their conference isn't selected. Because rankings shift weekly, the exact game count for Top 25 weeks isn't knowable in advance.
Exclude FCS GamesOnHides FBS-vs-FCS matchups, which are usually lopsided blowouts.
Max Point-Spread FilterOffLimits the picksheet to games within a spread range (3.0–21.0 points, in half-point steps) — keeps the picksheet from being dominated by obvious mismatches.
Exclude Specific TeamsNoneA block list of individual teams the commissioner never wants on the picksheet.
Select Specific GamesOffCommissioner hand-picks the exact games shown each week instead of using conference filters.
NoteThese six filters are configured on the same Picksheet & Eligibility tab as Team use limit and Pick-against team limit — they're listed separately here to call out that they're CFB/Combined-exclusive.
↑ Back to top

Quick Check

1. Which of these picksheet filters is exclusive to College and Combined contests?

6Contest Settings

Contest Settings — Pick Format

Pick Format controls how picks are scored, which direction entrants pick, and how many picks are required per slate.

Score type

OptionWhen a commissioner would choose it
Straight upPicks are scored on who wins outright. This is the classic, simplest Survivor format — recommended for casual or first-time pools since there's nothing to argue about beyond the final score.
Against the spread (Splash-set)Picks are scored against Splash-provided point spreads. Good for commissioners who want spread-based difficulty without having to manage numbers themselves.
Against the spread (Commissioner-set)Picks are scored against spreads the commissioner enters and manages manually. Useful for commissioners who want full control over the numbers (e.g. matching a Vegas line from a specific book, or intentionally adjusting difficulty).

Pick mode

Entrants can Pick winner (the team they expect to win each game) or Pick loser (the team they expect to lose each game) — a loser pool inverts the whole contest's risk profile, since picking a heavy favorite becomes the dangerous move instead of the safe one.

Picks per slate

Use 1 for classic single-pick Survivor. To require double (or more) picks in specific weeks, the commissioner uses the + and - controls next to each week to set the required picks — when multi-pick is active, entrants must make every required pick for that slate and all picks are graded independently.

Additional settings

  • Bye Pick — gives each member one optional bye per season. The member deliberately applies it to a specific slate to advance without submitting a pick. Off by default.
  • Picks in advance — allows members to submit picks for future slates, useful for planning around team-use limits. This is always on and can't be disabled.
↑ Back to top

Quick Check

1. Why might a commissioner choose Against the Spread (Commissioner-set) over Splash-set?

2. How does a commissioner set up a multi-pick week?

3. Is Picks in advance something a commissioner can turn off?

7Contest Settings

Contest Settings — Lives & Revivals

Lives & Revivals sets how ties are graded, how many losses an entry can survive, and what happens if an entire pool gets wiped out in the same week.

SettingDefaultWhat it does
Tie game outcomeLossHow a tie game is scored for the picked team. Cancelled games with no makeup inside the slate window are treated the same way — this setting decides whether the pick counts as a win or a loss.
Entry lives (a.k.a. mulligans)1How many losses an entry survives before elimination. The commissioner can raise this up to 10. 1 = instant elimination on the first loss.
Stop issuing lives after slateNone (no cutoff)Cutoff after which no more lives/mulligans are granted — can be set anywhere up to the CFB National Championship, useful for restricting mulligans to the early season.
Contest revivalsOffAuto-revives all entrants if every remaining entry is eliminated in the same slate. Revived entries keep their pick history with no extra lives — this prevents the whole contest from dying mid-slate.
NoteManual revivals and buybacks for individual entries aren't a contest setting — the commissioner handles those any time from the Entrants Manager after the contest is created (see Section 11).
↑ Back to top

Quick Check

1. What's the maximum number of Entry lives a commissioner can set?

2. What happens when Contest revivals is turned on and every remaining entry gets eliminated in the same slate?

3. Where does a commissioner go to manually revive a single eliminated entry?

8Contest Settings

Contest Settings — Missed Picks & Auto-Pick

This tab controls what happens when a member forgets to submit a pick before the deadline.

With Enable auto-pick on, the system fills missed picks automatically instead of leaving them blank (which would otherwise cost a life). Off by default.

Who sets the auto-pick logic?

OptionWhat it means
Commissioner sets for allOne auto-pick logic applies to every player in the contest.
Each player choosesPlayers are prompted to choose their own auto-pick preference when they join, and can update it from their entry settings at any time.

Team pick logic options: Home favorite (picks the home team when it's the spread favorite, falling back to the home team in pick'em games), Away favorite, or Random.

Maximum auto-pick events

Caps how many times a player can receive an auto-pick over the full contest — set to Unlimited, or Limited with a specific maximum count. Once the cap is hit, further missed picks cost a life (after any available Bye Pick).

↑ Back to top

Quick Check

1. What happens to a missed pick when Auto Picks is off?

2. Under 'Each player chooses,' when does a player set their auto-pick preference?

3. What does 'Home favorite' team pick logic do?

9Contest Settings

Contest Settings — Tiebreakers

Tiebreakers determine how the contest resolves if more than one entry is still alive at the end of the season.

SettingDefaultWhat it does
Tiebreaker ResolutionSplit (co-winners)How a final tie among alive entries resolves: every entry alive at the end wins together, or the commissioner enables a single-winner tiebreaker cascade instead.
Tiebreaker StackEmptyAn ordered, reorderable list of rules used to resolve to a single winner: Most Weeks Survived, Closest to Championship-Game Total (the entry's predicted combined score for the CFB National Championship, captured at entry creation), and Strength of Victory (SOV — cumulative win margin across the season; highest wins). The first rule that isn't a tie resolves the contest.
Last Entrant With Teams Remaining WinsOffIf exactly one entrant still has eligible teams while every other remaining entrant has run out, that entrant is declared the winner — no final correct pick required. Editable up to 48 hours before the first game; locked once the contest starts.
TipCo-winner split is how most people intuitively understand Survivor — everyone alive at the end wins. Only enable the tiebreaker stack if the group specifically wants a single champion.
NoteTiebreaker settings can be changed at any time, even mid-contest — unlike most other settings, there's no lock once the season starts.
↑ Back to top

Quick Check

1. What's the default Tiebreaker Resolution?

2. In the Tiebreaker Stack, what does 'Closest to Championship-Game Total' refer to for a CFB contest?

3. Can tiebreaker settings be changed after the contest has started?

10Contest Settings

Contest Settings — Groups & Design

Groups let a commissioner split a large pool into sub-standings (e.g. by department, region, or friend group); Design lets them brand the contest with a banner image.

Entrant Group

SettingDefaultWhat it does
Group standingsOffEnables grouped leaderboards and lets the commissioner choose how entrants join groups.
Group assignment modeCommissioner assigns groupsThree options: Commissioner assigns groups (entrants join without choosing), Entrants may choose a group (optional at join), or Entrants must choose a group (required before joining).
Contest groupsNoneAt least one group must be added before requiring entrants to choose one. Groups are added by name from this panel.

Design

Commissioners can upload a contest hero banner (PNG or JPG, up to 4 MB) to personalize the top of the contest page.

↑ Back to top

Quick Check

11Commissioner Tools

Entrants Manager & Invites Manager

These two commissioner-only tools handle everything about who's in the pool and how new members join.

Entrants Manager

The Entrants tab lists everyone in the contest with filter pills (All, Active, Missing picks, Removed, Paused, Notes, No notes) and a searchable table (entrant, name, email, entered date, entries, missing picks, notes). Click Download in the top-right to export the full roster.

From Email entrants, the commissioner can message the whole pool directly without leaving the contest.

Pick log

The Pick log is an audit trail: it shows each entrant's already-locked picks by week, filterable by slate. It deliberately hides live/future picks (as the on-screen banner notes) so the commissioner can confirm who picked what after the fact — useful for settling "did I actually submit a pick that week?" disputes — without ever revealing an entrant's in-progress or upcoming pick early.

Invites Manager

The New invite tab offers four ways to add people: Share link (copy a link to paste anywhere), Previous Splash entrants, Followers, and Import emails. Selected people appear in the "Invite" panel on the right, where the commissioner can optionally add a custom message before clicking Send invite. The Sent tab tracks everyone who's already been invited.

↑ Back to top

Quick Check

1. How does a commissioner export a full list of entrants?

12Standings

Leaderboards

Survivor has one season-long standings view — there's no weekly leaderboard, since the format is about survival, not points.

Ranking rules

  • Alive entries always rank above eliminated entries.
  • Among alive entries (when Entry Lives is greater than 1): more lives remaining ranks higher. Entries tied on lives are equal co-leaders.
  • Eliminated entries are ordered by elimination week — surviving longer ranks higher.
  • Buybacks and revivals never affect rank — a revived entry is treated identically to a clean entry at the same life count. Buybacks-used is shown as informational metadata only, not a ranking factor.
  • In a 1-life contest, all alive entries display as equal co-leaders until eliminated.

Filters

Standings can be filtered by search (entrant name), following, my entries, status (All / Alive only / Eliminated only), and by group when Groups & Design is enabled (Section 10).

How the contest resolves

By default the contest ends in a co-winner split — every entry still alive at the final week wins together. If the commissioner enabled the Tiebreaker Stack (Section 9), the standings instead resolve down to a single winner using whichever tiebreaker rule first breaks the tie.

NoteBecause there's no weekly leaderboard, entrants shouldn't expect to see a running point total anywhere in Survivor — the standings view only ever reflects alive/eliminated status and lives remaining.
↑ Back to top

Quick Check

1. How many leaderboard views does Survivor have?

13CS Reference

Missed Picks / Strikes Quick Reference

A fast reference for the most common Survivor support topics.

Missed pick (no submission by the deadline)

The fallback order is:

  1. Bye Pick (if enabled and unused) — consumed automatically, entry advances.
  2. Auto Pick (if enabled and under the Maximum Auto Picks cap) — the system fills the pick using the configured strategy.
  3. Otherwise — the entry loses 1 life (eliminated if that was the last one).
WarnAuto Picks default OFF in Survivor. If a group is expecting the platform to automatically cover missed picks, confirm the setting first — this is a common source of surprise eliminations.

Cancelled / postponed / suspended games

Postponed, suspended, and cancelled are one state in the system: cancelled. A cancelled game never gets rescheduled and stays in the same week. ("Delayed" is different — a delayed game still plays and scores normally.)

TimingWhat happens
Cancelled before kickoffThe pick stays editable until the pick deadline — the member just switches to another game. No penalty if they switch. If they don't switch by the deadline, the pick grades as a loss.
Cancelled after kickoff (includes suspended mid-game)The pick is frozen and grades as a loss.

Auto Pick never selects a cancelled game. The commissioner can modify picks after kickoff to give relief in an edge case — that's a release valve, not a setting.

"I ran out of teams to pick"

In team-use-capped pools, an entrant can pick themselves into a dead end — especially with multi-pick weeks or pick-against limits stacked on top of conference filters. There are no automatic guardrails; avoiding dead ends is the skill of Survivor. The commissioner can always loosen settings mid-season (e.g. raise the Team Use Limit, or broaden Select Conferences) to get the pool out of a jam.

Tie games

Graded per the Tie Game Outcome setting (Section 7) — Loss by default, Win if the commissioner chose that. Rare, but always contentious — point users to the contest's Lives & Revivals settings.

↑ Back to top

Quick Check

1. What's the correct fallback order for a missed pick?

2. What happens to a pick if the game is cancelled after kickoff?

3. Is there an automatic guardrail to stop an entrant from running out of eligible teams?

14Reference

FAQ

Q: What conferences can I include in a CFB Survivor picksheet?Any combination of SEC, Big Ten, Big 12, ACC, Mountain West, American Athletic, Conference USA, Mid-American, Sun Belt, and Independents — at least one conference must be selected.
Q: Can I run a pool that mixes NFL and college games?Yes — choose NFL + NCAAF Survivor at creation. Entrants pick from either league each week in the same contest.
Q: Does Include AP Top 25 Games guarantee a fixed number of games each week?No. Because rankings shift week to week, the number of Top 25 games on the picksheet isn't knowable in advance.
Q: My entry ran out of teams to pick — what now?There's no automatic fix built in. Ask the commissioner to loosen a setting mid-season — raising the Team Use Limit or broadening Select Conferences are the two most common fixes.
Q: Why didn't Auto Pick cover my missed pick?Auto Picks default OFF. Even when it's on, it stops working once an entry hits its Maximum Auto Picks cap, and it never selects a cancelled game.
Q: Can the commissioner see my future picks?Only if Hide Future Picks From Commissioner is turned off. It's on by default, meaning the commissioner sees picks on the same schedule as everyone else.
Q: What happens if everyone gets eliminated in the same week?If Contest Revivals is on, everyone is automatically revived with their pick history intact and no extra lives. If it's off, the contest resolves via the Tiebreaker settings instead.
Q: Is there a weekly leaderboard?No. Survivor only has one season-long standings view, ranked by alive/eliminated status and lives remaining.
Q: How many entries can one person have?1 by default. The commissioner can raise the per-person cap up to 250, but only before the contest goes live — it's raise-only once entries are open.
Q: How do I audit whether an entrant actually submitted a pick for a past week?Use the Pick log in Entrants Manager — it shows every locked pick by entrant and week without exposing anyone's live or upcoming picks.
↑ Back to top

Quick Check

1. Which contest format lets entrants pick from either league each week?

15Reference

Glossary

TermDefinition
EntryA single "ticket" in the contest, tracked separately from the member who owns it. A member can own multiple entries if Maximum entries per entrant allows it.
Life / MulliganA buffer against elimination. An entry starts with Entry Lives (default 1) and loses one each time it picks incorrectly.
Team Use LimitHow many times across the season a single team can be picked by the same entry.
Pick-Against Team LimitA cap on how many times an entry can pick against (as the opponent of) a specific team.
Bye PickA once-per-season free pass a member deliberately applies to a slate to advance without submitting a pick.
Auto PickAn automatic fallback pick applied after the deadline if a member forgot to submit one, using a configured Team pick logic.
Contest RevivalsAuto-revives every entrant if the entire pool is eliminated in the same slate.
Tiebreaker StackAn ordered list of rules used to resolve a multi-entry tie down to a single winner.
Strength of Victory (SOV)A tiebreaker rule based on cumulative win margin across the season; highest wins.
Playoff ResetResets team-use counts and pick history once the contest reaches the postseason.
Select Specific GamesA CFB/Combined-only picksheet mode where the commissioner hand-picks the exact games shown each week, instead of filtering by conference.
Pick LogAn audit trail of every entrant's already-locked picks by week, used to settle disputes without exposing live picks.
Co-winner SplitThe default end-of-contest resolution: every entry still alive at the final week wins together.
↑ Back to top
